linguisticChallenges.tokenization: Chinese has no spaces between words, making tokenization non-trivial. Word boundaries are ambiguous even for native speakers — '下雨天留客天留我不留' can be segmented multiple ways with different meanings. This affects how MT systems handle compound terms and placeholder insertion.
linguisticChallenges.measureWords: Chinese requires measure words (量词/classifiers) between numbers and nouns — 一个人 (one + generic + person), 一条路 (one + long-thin + road), 一把椅子 (one + graspable + chair). Using the generic 个 for everything sounds uneducated. MT systems frequently default to 个.
linguisticChallenges.simplifiedTraditionalConfusion: Models sometimes produce Traditional characters (繁體字) when Simplified (简体字) is requested, or vice versa. Single-character substitutions can slip through — e.g., 裡 (Traditional) vs 里 (Simplified). This is especially common with technical terms and proper nouns.
linguisticChallenges.registerVocabulary: Formality is expressed through vocabulary choice, not grammar. 你 (nǐ, casual you) vs 您 (nín, respectful you) is the main pronoun distinction. Four-character idioms (成语) convey erudition but can be opaque — use them sparingly in UI text. '请' (please) at sentence start marks formal register.
linguisticChallenges.aspectMarkers: Chinese expresses time through aspect markers (了 completion, 过 experience, 着 ongoing), not verb tense. Translating English past/present/future tense literally produces awkward Chinese. MT often inserts unnecessary temporal markers.
linguisticChallenges.fullwidthPunctuation: Chinese uses full-width punctuation (，。！？) rather than half-width (,.!?). Mixing half-width and full-width punctuation in the same text is a common MT error that is immediately visible to readers.

dialects.classification: Mandarin (Standard Chinese/普通话) based on Beijing dialect; mutually unintelligible dialect groups exist (Wu, Cantonese, Min, etc.) but share the writing system

encyclopedic.history: Simplified Chinese characters were promulgated by the PRC government in the 1950s-60s to increase literacy. The traditional characters remain in use in Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Macau. Both systems share the same grammar and vocabulary.
